九年前，我做出了我生命中最重要的决定 - 我转向所谓的古饮食（“Paleo”是有点误导，因为我在帖子中解释了。我没有这样做，我肯定会为美国的肥胖统计数据贡献。在转向古饮食的六个月内，我在当前重量平衡之前损失了20磅。但体重实际上是最不重要的事情。更重要的是，我在切换后几个月经历的我经历的一般健康的戏剧性提高。我今天比十年前感觉好多了，尽管已经（显然足够了）十岁了。
 在食物链上的重大变化很高。在纯粹的植物饮食中获得蛋白质的唯一方法是吃谷物和脉冲，这意味着小麦和豆类。但是，这些刻是消除的食物导致我的健康改善。当一家餐馆厨师为酱汁使用面粉时，我有时会在不知不觉中消耗少量小麦（尽管有明确的恳求不这样做）。第二天，我知道我被毒害了。
 另一件事是它不仅仅是蛋白质不足。你的身体不需要那种蛋白质。纯粹的植物饮食最大的问题是你没有得到足够的健康脂肪。相反，你最终用种子油毒害。看到脂肪的食物对您的健康有益，更有原因吃脂肪
 这就是我随着越来越多的警报观看当前趋势的原因，即“取消”肉。去年剑桥镇，英国两个最好的大学之一，禁止肉[更正：正如几位读者所指出的那样，这不是剑桥镇，而是从其自助餐厅拆除牛肉和羔羊的大学;我为不正确的陈述道歉！]。所以剑桥现在已经离开了我的地方（幸运的是，我多年前访问过，当时仍然是安全的）。我非常担心素食主义潮流将继续蔓延，让美国食肉动物预订（甚至在古饮食后灭绝的人）。
 有人肯定会立即指责我，我不关心环境。 Au对比。我见过的一些最令人沮丧的环境是巨大的农业领域（例如，穿过爱荷华州）。
        这是我喜欢生活的地方，如果实际上是可能的。这些是美观美观和生态的各种环境，这些环境将被作物单一栽培所取代，以便“在食物链上进食”。
 实际上，最近在美国往返草地的庄稼田地有一个安静的趋势。其中一些是由非营利组织完成的，就像美国野牛社会一样。他们想要更多的美国看起来像这样： 
还有个人农民切换到牧场。在碳牛仔队观看这部伟大的电影。
 缩短食物链的案例基于糟糕的科学。吃动物（不仅仅是肌肉，还有脂肪和器官）对你的健康有害吗？废话。这就是让我们的人类。这对环境不好？詹姆斯·莱茵河禁止肉类禁止肉类：
 谢谢@Cambridge_Uni禁止我的草喂牛肉 - 如何解决气候变化的主要原因，剥夺了化石燃料燃烧活动的投资组合，＆amp;改变你的17,000公顷的相当无菌耕地？这些是我的田野告诉我你的pic.twitter.com/eyf5mh9gom
  并且还有另一种意外的转换到植物饮食后果，我没有看到过多的讨论。在我的转向古饮食后，我注意到，令人惊讶的是，我的认知能力提高了我的认知能力。我的思想变得更加清晰，最引人注目，我的社会情报变得更加剧烈。当然，这是我们在科学中所说的那样完全“轶事”。但事实证明，这有一种声音科学依据。在少年监狱（例如）中进行的一些受控实验发现，囚犯补充ω-3的饮食的囚犯比对照条件的那些更具社会充分。当然，欧米茄3的最佳来源是草喂食反刍动物。或海鲜，但我们已经遗憾地污染了世界海洋的重金属，所以吃太多的鱼会导致砷中毒。
 回想起来，这些发现产生了很大的意义。毕竟，人类通过从大部分植物的饮食切换到强调肉食的饮食中，人类进化到成为“地球上最聪明的动物”。这是演变（并维持）大型人类大脑和高社会智能的唯一途径。
note_en:
Nine years ago I made one of the most consequential decisions of my life— I switched to the so-called Paleo Diet (“paleo” is a bit misleading, as I explain in the post). Had I not done so, I would certainly have contributed to the rising obesity statistics for the United States. Within six months of switching to Paleo diet I lost 20 pounds before equilibrating at my current weight. But weight is actually the least important thing. Much more important was a dramatic improvement of my general health I experienced in the months since switching. I feel better today than ten years ago, despite being (obviously enough) ten years older.
 The major change was eating much higher on the food chain. The only way to get protein on a purely plant-based diet is to eat grains and pulses, which means wheat and beans. But those are precisely the foods elimination of which resulted in my health improvement. I sometimes unknowingly consume small amounts of wheat, when a restaurant chef uses flour for the sauce (despite explicit entreaties not do so). The next day I know that I had been poisoned.
 The other thing is that it’s not just protein deficiency. Your body doesn’t need that much protein. The biggest problem with purely plant diet is that you don’t get enough healthful fat. Instead you end up poisoning yourself with seed oils. See  Fatty Foods Are Good for Your Health and  More Reasons to Eat Fat
 This is why I watch with increasing alarm the current trend to “cancel” meat. Last year the town of Cambridge, home of one of two best universities in UK, banned meat [ correction: as several readers pointed out it was not the town of Cambridge, but the University removing beef and lamb from its cafeterias; my apologies for the incorrect statement!]. So Cambridge is now off my list of places to go to (fortunately, I visited it years ago, when it was still safe for carnivores). I am very worried that the veganism tide will continue spreading, leaving us carnivores on reservations (or even driving people following Paleo diet to extinction).
 Somebody is sure to immediately accuse me that I don’t care about the environment.  Au contraire. Some of the most depressing environments that I’ve seen are giant agricultural fields (e.g. driving through Iowa) .
        This is where I would love to live, if it were practically possible. These are the aesthetically beautiful and ecologically diverse environments that would be replaced with crop monocultures to enable “eating lower on the food chain”.
 Actually, recently there has been a quiet trend in America towards replacing crop fields with grasslands. Some of it is done by non-profit organizations, like the  American Bison Society.They want more of America to look like this:
   And there are individual farmers who switch to ranching. Watch this great movie at  Carbon Cowboys.
 The case for shortening the food chain is based on bad science. Eating animals (not just muscle, but also fat and organs) is bad for your health? Bullshit. It’s what made us human. It’s bad for environment? Check out this response by James Rebanks to Cambridge University banning meat:
 Thanks  @Cambridge_Uni for banning my grass-fed beef – how about addressing the primary causes of climate change, divesting your investment portfolio of fossil fuel burning activities, & changing your 17,000 hectares of fairly sterile arable land? These are my fields Show me yours  pic.twitter.com/Eyf5mh9goM
  And there is another unintended consequence of switching to plant-based diet, that I haven’t seen much discussion of. After my switch to Paleo diet, I noticed, with surprise, that my cognitive abilities improved. My thinking became clearer and, most strikingly, my social intelligence became more acute. This is, of course, completely “anecdotal” as we say in science. But it turns out that there is a sound scientific basis for this. Several controlled experiments conducted in juvenile prisons ( for example) discovered that inmates whose diet was supplemented with Omega-3 were much more socially adequate than those in the control condition. Of course, the best source of Omega-3 is grass-fed ruminants. Or seafood, but we have sadly polluted the world ocean with heavy metals, so eating too much fish can result in arsenic poisoning.
 In retrospect, these findings make a lot of sense. After all, humans evolved to become the “smartest animal on Earth” by switching from largely plant-based diet to a diet emphasizing carnivory. This is the only way to evolve (and maintain) large human brains and high social intelligence.
title: “食物链上少吃的黑暗面”
title_en: The dark side of “eating lower on the food chain”

 window . addEventListener ( 'deviceorientation' , handleOrientation ) ; function  handleOrientation ( event )  {  const alpha  = event .alpha ;  const beta  = event .beta ;  const gamma  = event .gamma ;  // Do stuff... }
    But! Not every browser allows you to access the orientation data without user's permission. For example, in iOS 13 Apple has introduced the  requestPermission method. It must be triggered on user action (click, tap or equivalent).
  function  onClick ( )  {  if  ( typeof DeviceMotionEvent .requestPermission  ===  'function' )  {  // Handle iOS 13+ devices. DeviceMotionEvent . requestPermission ( )  . then ( ( state )  =>  {  if  (state  ===  'granted' )  { window . addEventListener ( 'devicemotion' , handleOrientation ) ;  }  else  { console . error ( 'Request to access the orientation was rejected' ) ;  }  } )  . catch (console .error ) ;  }  else  {  // Handle regular non iOS 13+ devices. window . addEventListener ( 'devicemotion' , handleOrientation ) ;  } }

note_en:
Published last month was an updated but still experimental version of  the native Wayland support for Wine after that code was originally published last year. One of the lingering questions has been around the prospects of mainlining this Wayland driver in Wine while last week the longtime Wine project leader, Alexandre Julliard, provided some clarity on the matter.   The engineers at Collabora have been making good progress on the Wayland driver for Wine to allow Windows games/applications to run on Wayland without having to go through XWayland while in the ensuing discussion on the latest version of the patches were the prospects or requirements around getting it accepted upstream.   Last week Alexandre Julliard as the project leader  commented on the matter. Ultimately he's open to it being upstreamed permitting that it uses established Wayland protocols and doesn't resort to relying on workarounds or other hacks that compromise the ability to maintain it.  I'm not opposed in principle to having a Wayland driver upstream. In fact I started writing one myself many years ago... It got stalled when I realized there was essentially no way to do decent window management, and that the best we could do would be the equivalent of X11 desktop mode, where we manage the windows ourselves. I don't have the impression that the situation has improved in the meantime, or that there is any interest in improving it.   That doesn't mean it couldn't go upstream, but then there will be constraints on what hacks and workarounds you'll be able to do. For instance, it will have to stick to protocols that are standardized across desktops, without adding compositor-specific workarounds, just like we don't allow window manager specific hacks on the X11 side otherwise it quickly becomes unmaintainable. ... I expect you'll find a much bigger impedance mismatch between Wayland and Win32, and that in the end you'll end up reinventing XWayland using Windows APIs. But if that's your idea of fun go ahead ;-)
  With the blessing that Julliard isn't outright against such a Wayland driver, the discussion so far is indicating it will likely go through Wine-Staging first before maturing and working its way to upstream.

 所以，当新的意大利总理马里奥·德拉希承诺扩大其前任“数字化”意大利基础设施并鼓励外国投资的工作，专家们批准。 Entrepreneur Matteo Berlucchi负责欧洲中央银行的前主任的德拉奇的英超，作为一个“令人难以置信的令人兴奋的机会”，而Vittorio Colao，前沃达丰高管和意大利的新的创新部长已经花了很多年度推文关于创新，或者，当他把它放在进口的Linkedin-ese中，“#innovation。”
 但是，虽然这是真实的意大利生活受到技术效率低下的影响 - 厂商不接受借记卡，但公交车站没有与他们在谷歌地图上的时间表中没有地系，当地政府似乎几乎无法使用电子邮件 -  Digitalizzaione Trape有多年来经过验证的是一个猥亵浪费的鸭肉，一匹被殴打，挖掘，复活，然后再次殴打。
   主要是，当新自由主义者谈论“数字化”时，这意味着投资未经证实的初创公司。这是2012年首次开始的趋势，当时Corrado Passera，然后是Technocrat Mario Monti的意大利经济创新部长，通过立法，向年轻人提供2亿欧元，并与年轻的税收激励措施一起举行，旨在“促进社会流动性“和”吸引外国人才和资本“。
 但是努力与硅谷竞争的数百万人显然浪费了数百万。虽然Venture Compling在此后长期发展，但在经济上没有翻译：离开该国的年轻人的数量继续飙升，而且意大利仍然作为一种技术回水而被涂抹 - 除了富裕的北方，也许是富裕的北方，大部分纳税人补贴的意大利“科技产业”是基于的。
 与此同时，随着技术人员和可用技术的数量增加，青年失业率无论如何，去年达到30％。尽管如此，政府在科技大学的一般方向上继续嘻嘻，特别是在大流行过程中，在此期间，它受到企业游说者联盟的影响。自2019年以来，全国创新基金，萨卡托运硕士（意大利政府银行）的投资臂（意大利政府银行）达到约2.45亿欧元，以超过10亿欧元的管理层，以超过480辆创业公司。 
但是最易于资助的企业的一瞥将谎言对数字化在风险资本家的底线上的刺激效果中产生任何社会利益的概念。在意大利风险资金的投资组合中 - 由于政府资金通常促成了 - 是提供如“通过将各种车辆转换为连通汽车的移动解决方案”和“帮助[ING]龙头企业提供这种令人眼花缭乱的有用服务的业务从任何类型的消费者数据中提取可操作的见解，在进行消费者体验时节省90％的时间。“讲述，由于他们的初始以来，所涉及的资金已经迅速发展;其余的经济没有。
 当然，数字化可以进入积极的变化。曾经有过良好的努力，以意大利官僚主义数字化，主要是通过SPID（公共数字身份制度），即七万百万意大利人所采用，尽管事实上，大多数公共机构都没有。同样地，Pagopa在线执行税务职能 - 再次，摄取是想要的。
  专用于数字化的欧盟恢复基金金额将进入公共场所，而不是私人金库仍然不清楚。但德拉奇领导了一个如此边缘化的联盟，意大利人留下了一个很好的交易，这肯定是最终结束，而不是初创公司和风险资本基金，而是“孵化器”和“创新中心”和“加速器”。
 前政府在Techno-Utopian幻想家的五星级运动中的狂热影响下 - 前创新的创新部长Paola Pisano是一名成员 - 以数字化的迷恋，以数字化的重点关注建造数字超级银行的大部分能源并推出信用卡现金返还方案，设法将拼命不足的大流行击中的医疗保健系统留下来。
 通常，当专家和政治家谈论数字化时，他们实际上正在谈论外国投资。对于美国巨型的近期令人难以置信的员工队伍背叛了一丝不苟。最近，在埃龙麝香的下一个大千兆夫妻 - 欧洲最大的新闻 - 已被批准在都灵省内建造。亚马逊也受到意大利的欢迎，尽管远非振兴技术部门，跨国公司更有可能进入小型竞争对手。
 “数字化”通过这种方式观看，在履行中心需要演出的展示股票经济工资奴隶制和未满足的工作。
 这是美国文化和经济垄断的疾病。在加利福尼亚州普遍凝视，意大利等国家觉得需要生产自己的类似硅谷。但是真正的硅谷不在乎，结果是一种绝望的，沐浴淘汰，水蛭可以用来习惯的钱和能量更好地让事情变得更好。

     If you could measure a country’s worth by the number of vapid start-ups it has produced, Italy would come up short. While Britain once produced  half a million mostly obscure technology companies over the course of a single year, Italy has only just edged  nine thousand in total.
 So, when new Italian prime minister Mario Draghi promised to expand on his predecessor’s work of “digitalizing” Italian infrastructure and encouraging foreign investment, pundits foamed approvingly. Entrepreneur Matteo Berlucchi  hailed the premiership of Draghi, the former chief of the European Central Bank, as an “incredibly exciting opportunity,” while Vittorio Colao, a former Vodafone executive and Italy’s new minister of innovation, had already spent pretty much the whole year  tweeting breathlessly about innovation, or, as he puts it in his imported LinkedIn-ese, “#innovation.”
 But while it is true that much of Italian life is blighted by technological inefficiencies — vendors don’t accept debit card, bus routes bear no earthly relation to their schedules on Google Maps, local government seems scarcely able to use email — the  digitalizzazione trope has proven over the years to be an obscenely wasteful canard, a dead horse that has been beaten, exhumed, revived, and then beaten again.
   Mostly, when neoliberals talk about “digitalization,” it means investing in unproven startups. It’s a trend that began in earnest in 2012, when Corrado Passera, then the Italian minister of economic innovation under the technocrat Mario Monti, passed legislation to deliver €200 million along with various tax incentives to young, underfunded startups, the  aim being to “promote social mobility” and “attract foreign talent and capital.”
 But the millions spent in an effort to compete with Silicon Valley were apparently millions wasted. While venture funding has  grown in the years since, that didn’t translate economically: the number of young people leaving the country  continued to soar, and Italy is still written off as a technological backwater — with the exception, perhaps, of the affluent North, where much of the taxpayer-subsidized Italian “tech industry” is based.
 Meanwhile, as the number of tech workers and available tech jobs  increased, youth unemployment rose regardless,  reaching 30 percent last year. Nevertheless, the government has continued to hip-spray money in the general direction of tech, especially over the course of the pandemic, during which it was under the influence of a consortium of  corporate lobbyists. Since 2019, the National Fund for Innovation, an investment arm of the Cassa Depositi e Prestiti (the Italian government bank), has committed around €245 million, out of over €1 billion under management, to over 480 startups.
 But the barest of glances at the sorts of businesses being funded puts the lie to the notion that digitalization produces any social benefit beyond its titillating effect on venture capitalists’ bottom line. In the portfolios of Italian venture funds — themselves often propped up by government funding — are businesses that provide such dazzlingly useful services as “offer[ing] mobility solutions by turning all kinds of vehicles into connected cars” and “help[ing] leading enterprises extract actionable insights from any kind of consumer data, saving them 90 percent of the time when doing research about the consumer experience.” Tellingly, the funds involved have grown  rapidly since their inception; the rest of the economy hasn’t.
 Digitalization can, of course, entail positive change. There has been a well-intentioned effort to digitize the Italian bureaucracy, primarily by means of the SPID (Public Digital Identity System), which seventeen million Italians have adopted — despite the fact that, unhelpfully, the majority of public bodies haven’t. PagoPA, similarly, performs tax functions online — again, though, uptake is wanting.
  How much of the EU recovery fund money earmarked for digitalization will go into public rather than private coffers remains unclear. But Draghi heads a coalition which has so marginalized the Italian left that a great deal is certain to end up not only with startups and venture capital funds but “incubators” and “innovation hubs” and “accelerators.”
 The previous government, under the dizzying influence of the techno-utopian fantasists of the Five Star Movement — the former minister for innovation Paola Pisano was a member — grew so infatuated with digitalization that, in focusing much of its energy on building digital super-banks and rolling out credit card cash-back schemes, it managed to leave the pandemic-struck health care system desperately underfunded.
 Often, when pundits and politicians talk about digitalization, they’re actually talking about foreign investment. The perennial pleas for American megacorps to annex Italy’s workforce betray more than a hint of desperation. Recently, there was much adulation upon news that Elon Musk’s next big Gigafactory — the biggest in Europe — had been approved to be built in the province of Turin. Amazon, too, has been  welcomed into Italy, although far from revitalizing the tech sector, the multinational is more likely to  eat into small competitors.
 “Digitalization,” viewed in this way, entails gig-economy wage slavery and unfulfilling work at the fulfillment center.
 Such is the disease of the American cultural and economic monopoly. Gazing wistfully at California, countries like Italy feel the need to produce analogous Silicon Valleys of their own. But the real Silicon Valley doesn’t care, and the result is a hopeless, sallow knockoff, leeching off the money and energy that could be used to actually make things better.

P-values are often misinterpreted or misused. My goal with this page is to explain p-values through an interactive simulation. (This is an early release that is still under development!).
  Written by  Kristoffer Magnusson, a researcher in clinical psychology.  You should follow him on Twitter and come hang out on the open science discord  Git Gud Science.
 This is an alpha version of this page, there might be some rough edges and missing features!
 P HACK: add 1 observations to all samples that do not show a positive effect and reanalyze.
 Please report errors or suggestions by  opening an issue on GitHub, if you want to  ask a question use GitHub discussions
 There are many ways to contribute to free and open software. If you like my work and want to support it you can:

      Steven Goodman pointed out years ago that p=0.05 corresponds to a likelihood ratio of about 3. Odds of 3:1 are not very impressive
 It isn't a *huge* prior -in the absence of good date, ie almost always, it's unreasonable to postulate a value >0.5
  I have read it several times. You say "This might only mean it’s worth getting more data to probe for a real effect.". That is how I would interpret p=0.05. But it's far from the interpretation used in practice.
 posteriors using p= have the same deficiencies & are completely distinct from our goals in hypotheses tests
 giving the null a huge spiked prior is radically at odds with how we understand null hypotheses, not to mention, they aren't proper frequentist priors. but we've been through this too many times.
 PPV is different. It uses the p-less-than definition for likelihood ratio which is inappropriate for tests designed to tell you whether an effect is chance or real.For that you need p-equals calculation of likelihood ratio (or BF)royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/full/10.10…
 The screening test analogy is not necessary, nor is it an exact analogy, for the reasons I just gave.
 The crucial assumption in my approach and several others has nothing to do with screening tests. Rather it's that it's sensible to test a point (or near-point) null. I think that that is often the case (but not always).
 this is according to YOU, but not according to people who know how to use p-values correctly & are not trying to fit hypothesis testing into the mold for PPV screening measures.
  The problem, surely, is that the simulation doesn't distinguish between type 1 error and false positive risk. So it doesn't really help people to understand what's bad about p values.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.108…andfpr-calc.ucl.ac.uk
 P p pppp p-values. Explained through simulations. (Simulations: the only way to understand frequentist statistics ... since simulations allow frequent repetitions.)
     So many people (students, lecturers & practitioners) really don't understand P values. This interactive simulation from @krstoffr should help make it a little clearer. I love how it features both p hacking & publication bias. rpsychologist.com/pvalue/
 Great illustration! Very timely, I can use it in my philosophy of science class today, where we discuss Carney's 'retraction' of her power pose paper (with Cuddy), and more generally how and why false positives appear in the literature.
 P p pppp p-values. Explained through simulations. (Simulations: the only way to understand frequentist statistics ... since simulations allow frequent repetitions.)
 Awesome! I really like that it integrates p-hacking (here optional stopping: sampling until significance is reached) to illustrate how it contributes to inflated results and, eventually, publication bias. I may borrow this for my teaching 😃
   Very nice! I really like how it features p-hacking (here in the form of sampling until significance is reached) to illustrate how it contributes to inflated results and, eventually, publication bias.

在世界大战之间，纳粹将在德国的自由地区开始争夺，然后将另一边的自卫作为暴力的流行，只有纳粹只能停止。
  今年早些时候，在右翼抗议者冲进国会大厦之后，右翼媒体声称他们已被“渗透到抗废物”，并且寻求正义的人被“分裂”。
 去年，在警察和右翼极端主义团体经常升级的黑人生活抗议活动中，右翼媒体一直辩护警察，并将BLM描绘为暴力，无所不能的威胁。
 去年（今年），一个最受保守的美国人的子集通过选择不认真地采取Covid-19来造成数十万人死亡，然后表现得好像真正的侵略者是授权面具的人民。
 几周前，德克萨斯州的电网被一个怪胎的冰暴，由化石燃料变得更糟，经过多年的保守，让这些网格过时和毫无准备。然后他们归咎于风力。
  20世纪50年代的保守政治家们在据称的“共产主义同情”中被袭来的人闯入了监狱，他们今天的学徒就像普遍的医疗保健一样，他们会迫使我们全部变成全面的社会主义，这是他们说的一个系统“从未在任何地方工作过。”当然，这仍然是他们自己记录了历史的公开攻击或覆盖了几乎每个社会主义国家。
  联邦法律痛苦地保护奴隶制，但却指责在暴政和侵略的北方。 
朝圣者烧毁了人们的巫婆，让美国原住民从自己的土地上出来，但只有在宗教迫害的基础上来到新世界之后。
 它不是修正主义者或偏见，说保守的白色美国人作为一个团体，已经表现出一种将自己视为受到一些暴虐的正统被压迫的失败者的模式，只能转向并肆无忌惮地强制执行更自主派。下次听到富有的白人谈论“SEDEADING” - 在开阔的海洋上建立新社会 - 或殖民火星，记住这一点。
  在过去十年或两个人中，这种在受害者造成其他人的模式时已经开始吃自己的尾巴。
 在他最近的独立特价，特别是在他最后的SNL独白，戴夫卡图莱指出，越来越多地轰动白人的问题，如阿片类药物，经济焦虑和警察残暴，是他们最初为少数群体创造的。其中许多人继续责备少数民族问题。
 更具体的例子：请记住推特当国会骚乱之后禁止特朗普？他的支持者尖叫着审查以及“奥威尔”如何“这一切是，尽管支持同一党，它支持了40多年的努力，让像推特这样的大公司，绝对自由做他们所做的事情。
  我以具体的原因提出这种友好的火力，它不会擦任何鼻子。丰富的自由派这样够了。这是令人厌恶的令人厌恶的是，他们最近有些人诽谤整个南方各国诽谤他们的科迪德和气候变化的问题，这是由特定选民的特定选民和一些腐败的政治家造成的。
 当你使用正确的用途的相同策略 - 简单来说，手指指向，Ad-Hominem攻击泥泞的受害者的水域 - 你毁了一切。你验证了正确的受害者复杂，并肯定了左边是一个愤世嫉俗的暴徒的错觉，攻击了正确的事情，而不是产生受害者的具体行动。 （我怀疑许多中间人自由主义政客，如他们的保守派同事，不希望人们知道真正的受害者和侵略者在我们的社会中。） 
不，我指出了这些保守派的实例在脚下射击自己，以暗示他们迫害叙事的最新恶棍的问题：“取消文化”。像往常一样，他们不是他们假装的受害者。
   让我们拿库存：我们失去了什么来取消文化？同盟雕像？ Jemima姨妈？红斯金斯吉祥物？富裕的纽约时报编辑当天的工作？
 另一方面，人们通常“取消”是什么？种族主义？ Transphobobia？同性恋恐惧症？开发穷人？法西斯同情？
 嘿，你猜怎么着？如果您赞同各种各样的历史，导致真正的侵略者和真正的受害者（实际上）的各种人和观点，那么人们会不同意你的意见。当大量的人不同意你时，你可能会觉得一个暴徒正在攻击你。塔达！
 在这种情况下，什么保守派和中间人越来越令人烦恼地做了，而不是承认他们所说的坏事，是武器的受害者。他们将其转化为一个尖刺的盾牌，以保护自己的不宽容观点，这是恰恰和完全的问题。
  我争辩的大多数“取消文化”是文化免疫系统的一部分。人们以广泛的陈述形式看到耐受性的威胁，并专门选择不容忍它们。
 如果我在网上表达意见并立即发现自己被互联网吓坏，由朋友抛弃，并从我的工作中解雇，我会发现它不公正和缺陷。但那是因为我不会向已经消除的群体表达传递或反犹太主义或其他不宽容情绪。 
一个人应该能够说，没有被矛盾，“纳粹遗失了”或“这是善良的联邦失去了”。这不是因为矛盾是不愉快的;这是因为与这些陈述相矛盾意味着您宣传犹太人或非裔美国人的不容忍。
 有一些“取消文化”采取了无情执行的现状，而不是保护宽容？绝对地。它发生在苏联俄罗斯。它碰巧伊斯梅梅尔威斯试图说服人们应该洗手。这是一个专制民主问题，也是侵略者 - 受害者问题，而不仅仅是左右问题。
 它最近发生在交易员乔的员工身上被解雇的令人醒目的罪行......带来与上层管理层的与工作场所有关的安全问题。
  发生这种情况时保守派在哪里？这是对的：他们在这篇推文中的回复中，告诉Bonnema他被解雇了。
 为什么正确的似乎关心取消文化，这么少的关于帮助贝尔诺米的人？答案应该更清楚。
 右边喜欢谈论他们是多么疲惫，他们是自由主义者“打击受害者”，感觉更好地被冒犯，或者更值得讲述。他们假装自己的受害者是真实的，但左边的受害者始终是一种心态，而不是客观现实。当然，他们这样做，所以他们的党和其在行业中的朋友可以继续，不拘从，使他人成为现实。
  有一个南公园的一集，兰迪沼泽得到了一个Dui，必须去酗酒匿名会议。听到他的饮酒是一种疾病，他是无能为力的控制，而且他需要对更高的力量充满信心，他刮着他的头，辞去了自己的轮椅，并声称他只能用明确的神圣行为治愈。 
这是左边的讽刺。有一些预测继续，也许是因为超级个人主义权利确实认为自己相对无能为力地解决大规模合作的大规模问题（即使你在一秒钟内看到，他们也阻止这种合作发生了） 。
 虽然正确担心别人感受到受害者的担忧，但左侧（真正的左侧，不是蓬松病和森松人和克林特郡，他们忙着拇指下降最低工资，并在没有警方改革的情况下在肯特的布料中跪在没有警察的情况下）关心最小化人们实际是受害者。个人责任可以帮助很多问题，但有时候，即使只有70-80％的人都要对此投票，所以也需要大规模的合作。
 佛罗里达州的孩子们在佛罗里达州的房屋将很快水下。有些孩子试图在Gofundme上为自己的救生手术提供资助。在街道上被警察在手腕上拍摄的警察，有非武装和非暴力的黑人。
  采取医疗保健。如果我们都支付彼此的健康保险，那么如果没有治愈，每个人都至少照顾了。不那么受害者。正确的“采取个人责任”和“自己”的学说只是意味着数百万人在生病或受伤时独自经历受害者（因为每个人最终都这样）。
 相反，左派是在国会的石墙中首次陷入困境，然后被右侧和中心描绘成自制受害者。解决大问题的努力被私人飞机航班和大型制药活动捐赠和警察联盟支持将受到影响。
  当正统促进残忍时，左侧想要挑战正统。当正统阻止它锻炼残忍时，太多的其他人想要挑战正统。关于自由的正确尖叫;左边理解自由和正义束缚在一起。
  一个快速提醒：当你推翻“正统”阻止残酷的时候，你就像纳粹和法西斯主义一样。你为强大的无法无天的。 
有些人认为这很好 - 它不值得拥有一个法律制度，该系统持有经济或政治强大的责任，就像其他人一样负责任。 “它违背了自然，”他们可能会说，“在弱者上的强大猎物。”
  事实上，在我们的本性中，合作和使用智能来增加整体福祉，因此我不会作为一个有效的论点招待。值得注意的是，有许多支持者存在，其中一些支持者仍然存在，并且仍然持有对我们国家的工作原理的影响很大。
 目标受害者也存在，这就是我们有法律的原因。拥有一定的规则（例如“没有谋杀”）实际上使社会更加自由。防止暂时强大的让受害者从暂时的无力创造信任。如果表格终于转变，动态将更加富有同情心和更少的报复。当我知道你以某种方式充分妨碍了傻瓜，我更愿意住在附近，并与你做生意。当人们打破这些规则时，需要在董事会中平等强制执行后果。
 为什么我在向社会的基础上解释基本数量？因为当社会忽视侵略者和受害者时，就不会发生正义。你不想生活在一个无法发生的社会中。那不是绝地思考。这只是事实。
note_en:
Between the World Wars, Nazis would start fights in liberal areas of Germany, then paint the other side’s self-defense as an epidemic of violence that only the Nazis could stop.
  Earlier this year, after right-wing protesters stormed the Capitol, the right-wing media claimed they had been “infiltrated by Antifa” and that the people seeking justice were being “divisive.”
 Last year, after police and right-wing extremist groups routinely escalated violence in Black Lives Matter protests, the right-wing media one-sidedly defended the police, and depicted BLM as a violent, omnipresent threat.
 Last year (and this year), a mostly-conservative subset of Americans caused hundreds of thousands of deaths by choosing to not to take COVID-19 seriously, then acted as though the real aggressors were the people mandating masks.
 A few weeks ago, power grids in Texas were brought down by a freak ice storm, made worse by fossil fuels, after conservatives fought for years to keep those grids outdated and unprepared. They then blamed wind power.
  Conservative politicians in the 1950s had people thrown in jail for alleged “communist sympathies,” and their apprentices today act like universal healthcare would force us all into full-on socialism, a system they say has “never worked anywhere.” This, of course, is in spite of their own documented history of overtly attacking or covertly sabotaging nearly every socialist country.
  The Confederacy fought bitterly to protect slavery, but accused the North of tyranny and aggression.
 The Pilgrims burned people for being witches and drove Native Americans out of their own lands, but only after coming to the New World on the basis of religious persecution.
 It’s not revisionist or biased to say that conservative white Americans, as a group, have shown a pattern of seeing themselves as underdogs oppressed by some tyrannical orthodoxy, only to turn around and murderously enforce doctrines even more radical. The next time you hear rich white people talking about “seasteading” — building new societies on the open ocean — or colonizing Mars, remember this.
  Over the last decade or two, this pattern of playing the victim while victimizing others has begun to eat its own tail.
 In his most recent standup specials, and especially in  his last SNL monologue, Dave Chappelle pointed out that several problems increasingly plaguing white Americans, like opioid overdoses, economic anxiety, and police brutality, are ones they initially created for minorities. Many of them continue to blame those problems on minorities.
 A more specific example: remember when Twitter banned Trump after the capitol riots? His supporters screamed about censorship and how “Orwellian” it all was, despite supporting the same party that has spearheaded more than 40 years of efforts to give big companies like Twitter the absolute freedom to do what they did.
  I bring up this friendly fire for a specific reason, and it’s not to rub any noses in it. Rich liberals do enough of that. It’s disgusting how gleefully some of them recently slandered entire Southern states for their problems with COVID and climate change, which were caused by a specific block of voters and a handful of corrupt politicians.
 When you use the same tactics that the right uses — simplistic, finger-pointing, ad-hominem attacks that muddy the waters of victimhood — you ruin everything. You validate the right’s victim complex, and affirm the illusion that the left is a cynical mob that attacks everything the right does, rather than specific actions that create victims. (I suspect that many centrist liberal politicians, like their conservative colleagues, don’t want people to know who the true victims and aggressors are in our society.)
 No, I point out these instances of conservatives shooting themselves in the foot to hint at the problem with the latest villain in their persecution narrative: “cancel culture.” As usual, they are not the victims they pretend to be.
   Let’s take inventory: what have we lost to cancel culture? Confederate statues? Aunt Jemima? The Redskins mascot? The day job of a  rich New York Times editor?
 On the other hand, what is it that people are generally “cancelled” for? Racism? Transphobia? Homophobia? Exploitation of the poor? Fascist sympathies?
 Hey, guess what? If you endorse the kinds of people and views that, throughout history, have resulted in real aggressors and real victims (millions, in fact), then people will disagree with you. And when large numbers of people disagree with you, you may feel like a mob is attacking you. Tada!
 What conservatives and centrists increasingly and infuriatingly do in this situation, rather than admit what they said was bad, is weaponize victimhood. They turn it into a spiked shield to protect their own intolerant views that are precisely and entirely the problem.
  Most “cancel culture,” I would argue, is part of a cultural immune system. People are seeing threats to tolerance, in the form of broadly intolerant statements, and specifically choosing not to tolerate them.
 If I express an opinion online and immediately find myself shamed by the Internet, abandoned by friends, and fired from my job, I’ll find it unjust and dystopian. But that’s because I won’t express transphobic or anti-Semitic or otherwise intolerant sentiments toward already-disenfranchised groups.
 One  should be able to say, without being contradicted, that “it’s good the Nazis lost” or “it’s good the Confederacy lost.” This is not because being contradicted is unpleasant; this is because contradicting those statements literally means you’re promoting intolerance of Jews or African-Americans.
 Has some “cancel culture” taken the form of ruthless enforcement of the status quo, rather than protecting tolerance? Absolutely. It has happened in Soviet Russia. It happened to  Ignaz Semmelweis for trying to convince people that surgeons should wash their hands. It’s an authoritarian-democratic issue, and an aggressor-victim issue, not simply a left-right issue.
 It recently happened to an employee at Trader Joe’s who was fired for the heinous crime of…bringing up COVID-related safety concerns about his workplace to upper management.
  Where were the conservatives when this happened? That’s right: they were in the replies to this tweet, telling Bonnema  he was right to be fired.
 Why does the right seem to care so much about cancel culture and so little about helping people like Ben Bonnema? The answer should be getting clearer.
 The right loves to talk about how tired they are of liberals “playing the victim” to feel better about getting offended, or more deserving of a handout. They pretend that their own victimhood is real, but victimhood on the left is always a state of mind, never an objective reality. They do this, of course, so that their party and its friends in industry can continue, undeterred, in making others’ victimhood a reality.
  There’s an episode of South Park where Randy Marsh gets a DUI and has to go to Alcoholics Anonymous meetings. Hearing that his drinking is a disease he’s powerless to control, and that he needs to put faith in a higher power, he shaves his head, resigns himself to a wheelchair, and claims he can only be cured with an explicit act of divine intervention.
  This is the right’s caricature of the left. There’s some projection going on, perhaps because the hyper-individualistic right does consider themselves relatively powerless to solve large-scale problems with large-scale cooperation (even if, as you’ll see in a second, they also block such cooperation from happening).
 While the right worries about other people feeling like victims, the left (the true left, not the Pelosis and Schumers and Clintons, who are busy thumbs-downing minimum wage hikes and taking a knee in kente cloths without passing police reform) cares about minimizing people actually  being victims. Personal responsibility can help many problems, but sometimes, that large-scale cooperation is needed, even if only 70-80% of people care enough to vote for it.
 There are kids in Florida whose homes will be underwater soon. There are kids attempting to fund their own lifesaving surgeries on GoFundMe. There are unarmed and nonviolent black people getting executed in the streets by cops who get a slap on the wrist.
  Take healthcare. If we all pay into each other’s health insurance, then everybody is at least taken care of, if not cured. Less total victimhood. The right’s doctrine of “take personal responsibility” and “get your own” just means millions of people go through victimhood alone when they get sick or hurt (as everyone eventually does).
 Instead, leftists are first stonewalled in Congress, then portrayed as self-made victims by the right and center. Efforts to address big problems are simply blocked by people from both parties whose private jet flights and Big Pharma campaign donations and police union support would suffer.
  The left wants to challenge orthodoxy when that orthodoxy promotes cruelty. Too many others want to challenge orthodoxy when that orthodoxy  prevents it from exercising cruelty. The right screams about freedom; the left understands that freedom and justice are bound up together.
  A quick reminder: when you overthrow “orthodoxy” that prevents cruelty, you get things like Nazis and fascism. You get lawlessness for the powerful.
 There are people who think this is good — that it’s not worth having a system of laws that holds the financially or politically powerful just as accountable as everyone else. “It goes against nature,” they might say, “where the powerful prey on the weak.”
  In fact, it is very much in our nature to cooperate and use intelligence to increase overall well-being, so I don’t entertain that view as a valid argument. It’s merely important to note that many proponents exist, and that some of them have held, and still hold, great influence over how our country works.
 Objective victimhood also exists, which is why we have laws. Having certain rules (e.g. “no murder”) actually makes a society much more free. Preventing the temporarily-powerful from making victims out of the temporarily-powerless creates trust. If the tables are ever turned, the dynamic will be more compassionate and less vengeful. And when I know you’re adequately deterred from sucker-punching me in some way, I’m more willing to live near and do business with you. And when people break these rules, consequences need to be enforced equally across the board.
 Why am I explaining what basically amounts to the foundation of society? Because when societies lose sight of who aggressors and victims are, justice can’t happen. You don’t want to live in a society where justice can’t happen. That’s no Jedi mind trick. That’s just facts.
title: 取消文化，保守的受害者和宽容的宽容
title_en: Cancel culture, conservative victimhood, and the paradox of tolerance

note_en:
iCloud has had the  occasional service issue, but its latest problem appears to be highly... specific. Actor and author Rachel True  claims iCloud has effectively locked her out of her account due to the way her last name was written. Reportedly, her Mac thought lower-case "true" was a Boolean (true or false) flag, leading the iCloud software on the computer to seize up. The problem has persisted for over six months, she said.
 True said she'd spent hours talking to customer service, and that Apple hadn't stopped charging her for service. She could switch to the free tier, although she'd also lose most of her online storage if she did.
   This is a rare flaw if it works as described. Even if you share the last name True (or False, for that matter), you'd have to type it a specific way to reproduce the problem. Still, it raises the question of how MacOS could mistake a name for a software flag in the first place. At least it doesn't appear to respond to direct commands — though you might want to get a name change if your surname is "rm rf," just to be on the safe side.
 Can get your coders to free my last name from icloud jail? Been locked out for 6+ months because of an uncapitalized t in TRUE, my surname but also a computer command.  Now that I a layman have explained problem to you a giant computer company, could u fix? @apple  @AppleSupport  https://t.co/TSEjUU1nXF
— Rachel True (@RachelTrue)  March 6, 2021
title: 据称，icloud锁定了姓氏是布尔值的用户
title_en: iCloud Allegedly Locked Out User Whose Last Name is a Boolean Value

在我生命的前34年，我总是每天吃三餐。我从未想过它 - 常规是令人满意的，它很容易进入我的生命，每天吃三餐就是美国人一般这样做的。然而，到去年夏天，那些数十年的习惯已经开始侵蚀。在家中工作并没有社会计划的时间盲目让我没有真正的理由在一天的任何具体时刻将冰箱覆盖到我的冰箱上。为了应付，我做了许多美国人在过去一年中所做的事情：我对新的常规进行了准有目的地摸索的，最终我想出了一些奇怪但是可行的结果 - 和大餐。
    大餐正是它听起来像的那样：一顿饭很大。它也从线性时间不受影响。大餐不是早餐，午餐或晚餐 - 社会结构，即不再存在于我的家中 - 尽管理论上可能在传统的时间内发生任何东西。当你准备好拥有它时，大餐来了，这只是你只能识别的那一刻。对我来说，这通常在下午晚些时候，但有时它是早餐。一般来说，每天一次发生一次。
 在节食（对不起，生物发布）趋势被称为间歇性禁食，人们将热量压缩到有限的时间窗口中。但这并不是大餐的全部。这不是饮食。每当我觉得有蛋白石杰克切片的IT-枝条，来自土耳其外卖场所的剩余的鹰嘴豆队有时会提供大餐，在勺子上的光滑花生酱。这句话始于一个关于我无法向朋友解释的笑话，为什么我在下午中旬制作意大利煨饭，或者为什么我没有答案“晚餐吃什么？”在下午6点。超越“呃，好吧，我在上午11点吃了一个巨大的卷饼，并在下午放牧，所以我想我已经完成了这一天。”现在我简单地说：“这是大餐的时候，”或“我已经有大餐”。
  我自己吃的好奇变化只是一个开始。大流行已经扰乱了日常生活的每一部分，但对人们如何吃的影响特别敏锐。用餐封闭件和周末无聊已经推动了一个驯养厨师的国家，以准备更多自己的饭菜。交付 -  App Middlemen抓住了抓住外卖市场的抓地力。供应短缺制作面粉，豆类，意大利面和酵母热商品。病毒食谱具有增殖 - 我可以在酵母，香蕉面包，青葱面食，烤制的羊肉或真正优质的铸铁锅比萨饼中感兴趣吗？
  即使对于过去一年的存在相对稳定的人而言，大流行的餐饮时间变化都是混乱的。这并不是说他们一直均匀消极。日常生活中的大转移有一种迫使人们进入新习惯的方式 - 并强迫他们弄清楚他们实际想要吃的东西。
  如果你从过去一年中厌倦了食品商业新闻，那么很少有人疑问，很多人以某种方式改变了他们的习惯。例如，许多人在1月份购买了更多的小吃，弗里托议会表示，其中一些面部护理品牌，如Tostitos和Lay，已经完成了销售额增加约为30％至40％的年份。根据一个市场分析，整个“水果零食”类别的销售额增加了一倍多。冰冻食品销售额增长了20％以上，而在线浇筑食品的在线订单，如口香糖和葡萄酒也有所不同，也有显着的增加。
  但销售数量和趋势报告只讲述了故事的一部分。在他们下面是人们试图将个人情况塑造到生存能力，或者甚至可能是愉悦，但是他们可以，最大的统一因素是“正常”不再存在。对于数百万人在大流行期间失去了收入，刚刚获得杂货往往是一个艰苦的胜利。在精美餐厅的富裕，恒定的鱼子酱送货上和私人安全的尿料泡沫中的小说让事情保持小说。中间的家庭争先恐后地形成了新的特质常规。 
Warning: Can only detect less than 5000 characters
您可能已经看到了故障线。当然，消失的通勤，遥控，以及在电话会议期间制作三明治的灵活性会改变人们的吃。创造了三级小的公理，以围绕围绕整个日子在其他地方努力工作的必要性，现在人们再次弯曲，围绕一系列新的挑战，围绕着人的生活。我们的旧饮食时间表比坐在一天内坐在卧室里更自然。
   但食物是一个充满感情的情绪主题，人们常常担心他们的行为变化 - 甚至那些觉得自然的人 - 是不管怎样的不健康。 Rachel Larkey是纽约Yonkers的注册营养师，专门从事大多数低收入客户中的饮食障碍的纽约，已经听到过去一年经常担心这一担忧。 “人们感觉像他们的日常生活现在是模特的，他们的一天没有很多结构，”她告诉我。 “如果我们有一个例程，我们的身体开始说，好吧，没关系;这是我的午餐时间。我现在饿了。“如果没有这种期望，人们会在当天的时间内注意到他们的饥饿，这是不一定的一天，或者在他们认为他们应该吃饭时，没有大部分食欲。
  这些挑战袭击了每个人的不同。如果您有钱购买厨房设备和食物，或者如果饮食不是一个压力，情感的雷区，则适应自己的转移需求更容易。但Larkey表示，关于“检疫15”的大部分曲率是愚蠢的。人们自然地获得和减肥，因为他们的生命变化的条件，并且现在对几英镑的极度反应可以复制大流行的其他压力对身心健康的危害。 Larkey告诉我什么，是你饮食习惯的变化让你感觉良好和健康 - 无论他们是否符合你的生活，那么你的需求比你以前所做的更好。
  新的或恶化的食物强制，例如进食远远超过你习惯的东西是警报的原因。但是，雷克朗说，闹钟没有引起的，是调整的饮食模式或午餐时间，这些东西更有用或在奇怪的，压力条件下的人现在生活中的奇怪。“我们真的没有教导我们可以相信我们的身体提示，”她告诉我。 “这可能会令他们想到他们第一次来思考他们可能会稳定。”
 在一些新的例程中创造的，使过去的年度不那么繁重，这并不难看大流行后的寿命可能会更加灵活地为烹饪和吃东西做出更加灵活的人。但是，现在，继续前进，做任何感觉对。如果你在午餐时没有饿，没有理由窒息你的早晨希腊酸奶，或者当你骨头疲惫时强迫自己做饭，并且对奶酪和饼干很满意。你可能不会一直到大餐，但你不必在早餐，午餐和晚餐时陷入困境。

For the first 34 years of my life, I always ate three meals a day. I never thought much about it—the routine was satisfying, it fit easily into my life, and eating three meals a day is just what Americans generally do. By the end of last summer, though, those decades of habit had begun to erode. The time-blindness of working from home and having no social plans left me with no real reason to plod over to my refrigerator at any specific hour of the day. To cope, I did what many Americans have done over the past year: I quasi-purposefully fumbled around for a new routine, and eventually I came up with some weird but workable results—and with Big Meal.
    Big Meal is exactly what it sounds like: a meal that is large. It’s also untethered from linear time. Big Meal is not breakfast, lunch, or dinner—social constructs that no longer exist as such in my home—although it could theoretically occur at the traditional time for any of them. Big Meal comes when you’re ready to have it, which is a moment that only you can identify. For me, this is typically in the late afternoon, but sometimes it’s at breakfast. Generally, Big Meal happens once a day.
 In the dieting (excuse me,   biohacking) trend known as intermittent fasting, people compress their calories into a limited window of hours. But that’s not what Big Meal is at all. It’s not a diet. I snack whenever I feel like it—Triscuits with slices of pepper jack, leftover hummus from the Turkish takeout place that sometimes provides Big Meal, a glob of smooth peanut butter on a spoon. The phrase started as a joke about my inability to explain to a friend why I was making risotto in the middle of the afternoon, or why I didn’t have an answer to “What’s for dinner?” at 6 p.m. beyond “Uh, well, I ate a giant burrito at 11 a.m. and grazed all afternoon, so I think I’m done for the day.” Now I simply say, “It’s time for Big Meal,” or “I already had Big Meal.”
  This curious change in my own eating was just the beginning. The pandemic has disrupted nearly every part of daily life, but the effects on how people eat have been particularly acute. Dining closures and weekend boredom have pushed a country of reticent cooks to prepare more of its own meals. Delivery-app middlemen have tightened their grip on the takeout market. Supply shortages have made flour, beans, pasta, and yeast hot commodities. Viral recipes have proliferated—can I interest anyone in  sourdough,  banana bread,  shallot pasta,  baked feta, or a  truly excellent cast-iron-pan pizza?
  Even for people who have had a relatively stable existence over the past year, pandemic mealtime changes have been chaotic. Which isn’t to say that they’ve been uniformly negative. Big shifts in daily life have a way of forcing people into new habits—and forcing them to figure out what they actually want to eat.
  If you pore over the food-business news from the past year, there’s little question that  lots of people have changed their habits in one way or another. For instance, many people are buying more snacks—in January, Frito-Lay said that some of its marquee brands, such as Tostitos and Lay’s, had  finished the year with sales increases of roughly 30 to 40 percent. The entire “fruit snack” category has  more than doubled its sales, according to one market analysis. Frozen-food sales are up  more than 20 percent, and online orders of packaged foods as varied as chewing gum and wine have also seen a  marked increase.
  But sales numbers and trend reports tell only part of the story. Underneath them are people trying to mold their individual circumstances to survivability, or maybe even pleasure, however they can, and the biggest unifying factor is that “normal” hardly exists anymore. For millions of people who have lost income during the pandemic, just getting groceries is often a hard-fought victory. Among the wealthy, constant Caviar deliveries and access to private, pandemic-safe dining bubbles at fine restaurants have kept things novel. Households in the middle have scrambled to form new, idiosyncratic routines all their own.
  Wendy Robinson, a community-college administrator in St. Paul, Minnesota, told me that working from home most of the week has had the opposite effect on her than it did on me: It added  more meals to her life. Before the pandemic, “a lot of my eating was really convenience-driven, and I didn’t have a dedicated lunchtime, because I just was so busy,” she said. Food came erratically—from a co-worker’s desk, from the campus cafeteria, from Starbucks, picked up on the way home after a late night at work. Now she eats a real lunch most days, and she cooks more—a hobby she has always enjoyed—because she can do it while she’s on conference calls and during what used to be her commute.
  Kids have necessitated their own set of pandemic adaptations. Robinson and her husband, who also works from home most of the time, have two kids who attend school remotely. Despite a rough first few months and plenty of ongoing stresses, Robinson says the at-home life has also given her more opportunity to cook with her kids and teach them the basics. Lately, her 12-year-old son has begun to enthusiastically pitch in during the family’s meals. “He makes a legit great omelet and delicious scrambled eggs, and he makes himself grilled cheese,” Robinson said. “Sometimes, when I am really busy, he will make me lunch now.”
 With younger kids, things can be a little trickier. Scott Hines’s sons, 4 and 5, aren’t yet old enough to manage many cooking tasks for themselves, but they are old enough to seek out munchies. “I swear there are days where they’ve eaten snacks and no meals,” Hines, an architect based in Louisville, Kentucky, told me. “The days that they’re doing online learning, it’s impossible to control that, just because they’re bored.” On the upside, Hines, an enthusiastic cook who runs a newsletter for sharing his favorite recipes, said that working from home for part of the week has allowed him to try more types of cooking projects this year. Before, he often relied on foods that could be microwaved or otherwise prepared quickly. Now, he said, “I can make a soup; I can make something that goes in the pressure cooker or sits in the Dutch oven for hours, because I can start it at lunchtime.”
   For people without kids, and especially those who live alone, the pandemic’s impact works out a little differently in the kitchen. When it’s just you, there’s no bugging your partner to wash the dishes or trading off cooking duties with a roommate or letting a budding teen chef chop the vegetables. It’s all you, every time you’re hungry. “The amount of effort is immense,” Ashley Cornall, a 30-year-old project manager in San Francisco, told me. “It’s spending my entire life washing dishes, or in my kitchen, prepping something.” Before the pandemic, many of Cornall’s meals were social occasions, or something quick picked up from the zillions of restaurants built to feed the Bay Area’s office workers in their offices. She still orders takeout occasionally, but often feels bad about asking a delivery person to ferry food to her. Because constant Zoom meetings during the day make it hard to slip out to pick something up, she tends to find herself cobbling together a meal out of snacks.
  Even so, Cornall told me she has grown to enjoy cooking when she does have the time for it. “There is something kind of nice about putting on music and cooking a meal in the evening and having half a glass of wine, taking a moment to enjoy it,” she said. Having more control over what’s in her food has also helped her get closer to a longtime goal of switching to vegetarianism; she’s not totally there yet, but she eats a lot less meat than she used to.
  Splintering the three-meals-a-day norm might at first feel unnatural, but in the long arc of human history, that eating schedule is both extremely recent and born almost entirely of social convenience. According to Amy Bentley, a food historian at NYU, eating three meals a day is not something we do because of nutritional science or a natural human inclination. Instead, it’s largely a consequence of industrialization, which formalized the workday and drew much of the population away from home on a regular basis. Preindustrial America was more rural and agrarian, and people worked during daylight hours, pausing midmorning and later in the afternoon. “It was more like a two-meal kind of schedule that was based on outdoor physical labor and farm labor, and those meals tended to be quite big,” Bentley told me.
  Over time, more and more Americans were drawn into daily life outside the home—more kids were sent to school, and housewives and domestic workers, whose presence was once common in middle-class American homes, joined the formal labor market. Industrialized food processing began to provide an array of products marketed as  quick-and-easy breakfast foods—products that had never previously existed but whose ubiquity accelerated after World War II. Industrialized breakfasts such as cornflakes and instant oatmeal make for meals that are generally small and nutritionally hollow, which meant that people then needed to eat again during the day before commuting home for a later dinner, which was—and often still is—important for its role in family social life.
 You can probably see the fault lines already. Of course vanishing commutes, remote schooling, and the flexibility to make a sandwich during a conference call would change how people eat. The three-meal-a-day axiom was created to bend human life around the necessity of leaving the home to work elsewhere for the whole day, and now people are bending once again, around a whole new set of challenges. Our old eating schedules are no more natural than sitting in a cubicle for 10 hours a day.
   But food is a fraught emotional topic, and people often worry that changes in their behavior—even those that feel natural—are somehow unhealthy. Rachel Larkey, a registered dietitian in Yonkers, New York, who specializes in treating eating disorders among her mostly low-income clients, has heard this worry frequently over the past year. “Folks are feeling like their routines are kind of nebulous now, and they don’t have a lot of structure in their day,” she told me. “If we have a routine, our body starts to say,  Okay, it’s noon; it’s my lunchtime. I’m hungry now.” Without that expectation, people notice their hunger at hours of the day that aren’t necessarily mealtimes, or find themselves without much of an appetite when they think they’re supposed to eat.
  These challenges hit everybody differently. Adapting to your own shifting needs is easier if you have money to buy kitchen equipment and food, or if eating isn’t a stressful, emotional minefield for you. But Larkey said that much of the scaremongering about the “quarantine 15” is silly. People naturally gain and lose weight as the conditions of their life change, and extreme reactions to gaining a few pounds right now can compound the harm of the pandemic’s  other stresses on physical and mental health. What matters, Larkey told me, is whether the changes in your eating habits make you feel good and healthy—whether they fit your current life and your needs better than what you were doing before.
  New or worsening food compulsions, such as eating far more or far less than you used to, are cause for alarm. But what’s not cause for alarm, Larkey said, is adjusted eating patterns or mealtimes that are more useful or satisfying in the weird, stressful conditions people are now living in. “We’re really not taught that we can trust our body’s cues,” she told me. “It can feel so destabilizing to have to think about them for maybe the first time ever.”
 In some of the new routines created to make the past year a little less onerous, it’s not hard to see how life after the pandemic might be made a little more flexible—more  humane—for tasks as essential as cooking and eating. For now, though, go ahead and do whatever feels right. There’s no reason to keep choking down your morning Greek yogurt if you’re not hungry until lunch, or to force yourself to cook when you’re bone tired and would be just as happy with cheese and crackers. You might not make it all the way to Big Meal, but you don’t have to be stuck at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.
title: 没有真正的理由每天吃3餐
title_en: There’s No Real Reason to Eat 3 Meals a Day

跳到主要内容比特币，以外源等加密货币已经进入了主流话语，但他们也被广泛传播的概念加入，但理解得很差：“块区”或“区间”。 BlockChain的想法，加密增强的数字分类帐，底部和大多数加密货币如何，现在用于描述来自银行间交易的系统到沃尔玛的新供应链数据库。这个术语变得如此普遍，这很快就会失去意义。
 “什么是'区块链'？这个词是一个越来越多地玷污的流行语，“David Gerard，50脚块链的攻击作者：比特币，区块，以amp;智能合同，在电子邮件中表示。
 文本，音频和视频中有无数区块链接器。几乎所有这些都是错误的，因为他们从错误的前提开始。没有一个普遍的区块链定义，并且存在普遍的分歧，以便称之为区块链条的质量至关重要。
  比特币制度被认为是第一个区块链 - 这是推出Blockchain行业的Epiphany，这些支持者认为，支持者将彻底改变金钱，政府及以后。
 比特币旨在公开，允许任何人加入，它的区块链诞生了不需要让人们在没有中央权威的情况下保持诚实。设计牺牲了效率，以确保盗窃不会付出代价，因为重写分类帐需要这么多的计算能力，它比任何潜在的上行都更昂贵。为了达到这种效果，比特币区块链由数字分类帐组成，该数字分类帐将从时间开始到现在的所有交易。分类帐的副本不存储在中央位置;相反，它们被称为“节点”的超级用户保存。这些节点中的一些名为“矿工”，批量交易并将其添加到“块”中将其加密链接到所有先前块中的每个块。奇迹般地，该系统与来自核心比特币开发团队的管道相结合，已经运作了近10年。
 根据IBM的说法，比特币在2009年在野外首次亮相，“是第一次实施区块链技术”。然而，许多标记为“区块链”的技术设计今天与比特币的区块链没有相似之处。
  谷歌对“区块链”的定义是“一个数字分类帐，其中在按比较和公开的比特币或其他加密货币中制作的交易。”虽然大多数人都同意区块链是数字分类帐，但许多区块链没有相关的加密电流，并且没有公开录制。有些人甚至会争辩说区块链不需要数字。 
Investopedia说，“一个区块链是所有加密货币交易的数字化，分散，公共分类账。”同样，许多区块链不公开，许多其他人没有分散。
 IBM的定义说，“区块链技术用于各方的对等网络，他们都参与了给定的交易。”除此之外，至少是一个公开的区块链，除了世界食品计划建造的一个良好的区块链，只有一个参与派对：本身。 IBM继续下去：“因为分类帐分布，所涉及的每个人都可以在任何时间点看到”世界州“，可以监控交易的进度。”然而，万事达卡的区块链是任何人都不可见的（并且在MasterCard承认付款仍然通过其现有系统运行时，营销范围内没有任何功能）。
  高度上市的爱沙尼亚的努力提供了术语“区块链”的延伸和稀释的一个很好的例子。 “自2007年以来，爱沙尼亚一直在经营普通的国家数字身份方案，”哈佛商业评论去年写道。纽约人于2017年12月写道，“爱沙尼亚的数字安全的骨干是一个区间的技术。”
 爱沙尼亚的系统实际上预测比特币区块链，并对它是否应该被称为区块科技进行一些分歧。
 David Birch，Fintech顾问和Babylon之后的作者超越了比特币，发现自己是爱沙尼亚的Cio，Siim Sikkut，似乎证实爱沙尼亚的系统不是区块链。
 “我问他在其中'爱沙尼亚区块的ID'神话来自于我发现这个城市传奇已经获得了这种牵引力的绝对困惑，”桦树写道。“ “他说，与人们误解使用哈希保护的人来保护爱沙尼亚系统中的数据完整性可能有关。”
 爱沙尼亚的技术供应商，保护时间从“Hash-Conted Time-Stamping”到“区块链技术”。这不一定是不真实的，因为“BlockChain”没有关于定义 - 而且现在，这是一个很好的营销策略。 
“我问他在那个'爱沙尼亚区块的身份的神话中，因为我发现这个都市传说已经取得了这种牵引力绝对困惑。”
 “我们一直在努力工作，长时间在比特币被思考，”监护台首席执行官Mike Gault在一封电子邮件中表示。 “比特币没有新的加密 - 它背后的天才正在采取不同的加密构建块，并建立一个加密货币协议，激励人们使用它。”
 BlockChain是“只有附加的数据结构，其中包含密码链接在一起的数据记录”，“他说。 “当多个分布式方根据预定的规则达成共识时，数据记录将添加到数据结构中。”
  像金融业所提出的那样的新区块提案一大堆是所谓的“私人”区块。批评者表示，这些项目是伪装成新技术的旧技术。
 “私人区块链”只是共享数据库的混乱名称，“普林斯顿助理计算机科学教授arvind Narayanan写道，他们在Coursera上教授一个受欢迎的区块链课程。
 Narayanan认为，比特币区块链背后的关键创新是所谓的工作证明机制，旨在取代中央权威的必要性，规则和激励措施将使网络成员诚实。工作证明效率低下，是比特币的网络消耗这么多能量的原因，所以这并不一定是抛弃它的坏事。但是没有工作证明，那么区间有什么样的东西吗？
 “”私有区块链“只是共享数据库的令人困惑的名称。” 
有些人会引用其他加密技术，作为区块链和“vanilla共享数据库”之间的区分，Narayanan写道，但这些技术没有任何新的。 “加密使系统更加努力地篡改，更容易审计，”他写道。 “但这些方面的树木截止而不是比特币的创新！事实上，Satoshi略微从早期的研究中略微调整它们，他在他的白皮书中引用 - 通过Haber和Stornetta研究一路返回1991年！“
  这种不确定性通过膨胀了“区块链”项目的数量并夸大了该技术的能力来促进行业的一般吸水性。随着国家通过区块链相关的立法，将来也可能导致未来不可预测的问题。
 伦敦大学学院大学学院区区块科技厅的圣玛丽大学法学院副教授Angela Walch撰写了一篇关于区块链相关的术语和法律的论文。
 “一群国家真的急于通过一些立法来证明他们是如何过度友好的或科技所在，”她说。 “他们中的许多人正在将区块链技术的定义放在这些法规中，从我的角度来看，它们是非常有问题的定义。”
 威尔奇界最多的定义是由亚利桑那州开发的。亚利桑那州的电子交易法于2017年修订，澄清它涵盖了在区块链中完成的交易。在这样做时，立法机关写了一个定义：“'区块链技术'意味着分布式分类帐技术，使用分布式，分散，共享和复制的分类帐，这可能是公共或私有的，许可或许可，或者由令牌加密经济学驱动或驱动的。分类帐上的数据受到密码学的保护，是不可变和可审计的，并提供未经审查的真理。“
 仅仅因为数据处于区块链并不意味着数据是准确的
 特别是，沃尔奇对短语“不可变”和“未经审查的真理”担心，这将绝对归因于可能被描述为“难以改变”和“未经审查的人，只要维持网络，他们可能包括矿工，开发人员或独裁者，希望它成为。“ GIDEN GREENSPAN，COIN SCIENTS的创始人写道，重写比特币区块的成本在达到的攻击中，达到了激励的国家。有许多高度公布的区块链实例被改变：比特币已经多次分叉，包括2010年，当软件中的“整数溢出”错误导致创建920亿比特币和整个网络时必须回滚分类账。在2016年巨大的黑客之后，Ethereum被叉了。此外，由于欧洲的一般数据保护监管，这在5月生效并表示用户必须控制他们的数据，开发人员现在正在探索从区块链中删除数据的方法。 
短语“未经审查的真相”也忽略了这个事实，因为数据处于区块链并不意味着数据是准确的。不准确的数据，例如医疗记录的错误，仍然可以在区块链中验证。
 “当该定义没有与技术的相似之处时，法院应该做什么？从法律角度来看，这是什么意义？“沃尔奇说。 “事情会变得非常凌乱。”
 让事情变得更糟，亚利桑那州的定义现已用于其他国家在内的拟议立法。在200多个政府中也提出了区块的设计，以用于各种应用程序，包括投票，财产记录和数字身份。
  Victoria Lemieux是英国哥伦比亚大学集团科学委员会和区区研究集群的副教授，正在努力为国际标准组织制定区块链术语标准。
 “一般来说，如果事务在块中收集在一起，并且它是使用密码术的链条上固定的块，并且它被设计为防篡改并产生不可变记录，该系统有资格作为区块链，”她说在电子邮件中。 “表示，在一般用法，区块链通常是一个术语，即使事务未被组织成块。”
  她的团队遇到了一些挑战，包括“不同的认知社区已经形成了他们自己关于区块链的想法，一些具有非常强烈的政治和社会观点，这些思想在开源，分享和自主中非常强烈。”她说，这些社区并不融入ISO过程中，许多成员认为他们被大型技术公司和其他商业利益所掩盖。
 另一项挑战是“可能意味着这些司法管辖区失败，并使该技术的法律程序或应用复杂化，”她说。 
Lemieux也很好地熟悉对块链的能力的误解。 “值得信赖的概念 - 至少来自档案科学的观点 - 远远超出了区块链可以做些什么，甚至在大多数情况下要做的事情，”她说。这个想法意味着记录是准确的，“这不是一个通常在众多区块链解决方案的范围内的东西”并夸大了他们的可靠性，这是一个“问题，如果您有智能合同或新颖的智能合同和未经证明的共识算法”。它还夸大了真实性的声明，依赖于与区块链配对的任何身份系统的鲁棒性。 “最后，不可动提器意味着持久性，并且没有保证创造并保持链子的分类记录将持续下去，即使有很多副本，因为技术过时以及让系统的激励措施可能会在一段时间后消失， “ 她说。
 建立明确的定义将有助于清除一些误解。 “制定更精确的对区块链技术的理解将有助于我们解决其缺点并提高它，以便更好地以其支持者想要的变革方式，”她说。 “当我们谈论它时，我们难以推进技术或使用它时，很难进行对话。”不幸的是，她估计术语标准将需要大约18个月才能完成。
 科技加利福尼亚州为迪士尼乐园和其他主题公园提供了重新打开4月1日的限制

note_en:
Skip to main content                                                                                                                Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other cryptocurrencies have entered the mainstream discourse, but they’ve also been joined by a concept that is widely circulated, but poorly understood: “the blockchain” or just “blockchain.” The idea of a blockchain, the cryptographically enhanced digital ledger that underpins Bitcoin and most cryptocurrencies, is now being used to describe everything from a  system for inter-bank transactions to a  new supply chain database for Walmart. The term has become so widespread that it’s quickly losing meaning.
 “What is a ‘blockchain’? The word is a buzzword that is increasingly ill-defined,” David Gerard, author of  Attack of the 50 Foot Blockchain:  Bitcoin, Blockchain, Ethereum & Smart Contracts, said in an email.
 There are countless blockchain explainers in text, audio, and video around the web. Almost all of them are wrong because they start from a false premise. There is no universal definition of a blockchain, and there is widespread disagreement over which qualities are essential in order to call something a blockchain.
  The  Bitcoin system is considered the first blockchain — the epiphany that launched the blockchain industry that proponents say will revolutionize money, government, and beyond.
 Bitcoin was designed to be public and allow anyone to join, and its blockchain was born out of the need to keep people honest in the absence of a central authority. The design sacrificed efficiency in order to ensure that theft wouldn’t pay because rewriting the ledger would require so much computational power that it would be more costly than any potential upside. In order to achieve this effect, the Bitcoin blockchain consists of a digital ledger that records all transactions from the beginning of time to the present. Copies of the ledger are not stored in a central place; instead, they are kept by superusers called “nodes.” Some of these nodes, called “miners,” batch transactions and add them to the ledger in “blocks,” cryptographically linking each block to all the previous blocks. Miraculously, this system, combined with stewardship from the core Bitcoin development team, has functioned for almost 10 years.
 Bitcoin, which debuted in the wild in 2009, “is the first implementation of blockchain technology,”  according to IBM. And yet, many of the technology designs that are labeled “blockchain” today bear little to no resemblance to Bitcoin’s blockchain.
  Google’s definition of “blockchain” is “a digital ledger in which transactions made in bitcoin or another cryptocurrency are recorded chronologically and publicly.” While most people would agree that a blockchain is a digital ledger, many blockchains  do not have an associated cryptocurrency and are  not recorded publicly. Some would even argue that a blockchain  needn’t be digital.
 Investopedia says, “A blockchain is a digitized, decentralized, public ledger of all cryptocurrency transactions.” Again, many blockchains are not public, and many others are  not decentralized.
 IBM’s definition says, “Blockchain technology is used in a peer-to-peer network of parties, who all participate in a given transaction.” Except that at least for one well-publicized blockchain, the one built by World Food Programme, there is  only one participating party: itself. IBM goes on: “Because the ledger is distributed, everyone involved can see the ‘world state’ at any point in time and can monitor the progress of the transaction.” Mastercard’s blockchain, however, is not viewable by anybody (and seems to have no function outside of marketing since Mastercard admits that payments are still  running through its existing system).
  Highly marketed efforts in Estonia provide a good example of how the term “blockchain” has been stretched and diluted. “Since 2007 Estonia has been operating a universal national digital identity scheme using blockchain,” the  Harvard Business Review  wrote last year.  The New Yorker  wrote in December 2017 that “the backbone of Estonia’s digital security is a blockchain technology.”
 Estonia’s system actually predates the Bitcoin blockchain, and there is some disagreement over whether it should be called a blockchain technology.
 David Birch, a fintech consultant and author of  Before Babylon, Beyond Bitcoin, found himself at a blockchain event with Estonia’s CIO, Siim Sikkut, who seemed to confirm that Estonia’s system is  not a blockchain.
 “I asked him where this ‘Estonian blockchain ID’ myth came from since I find it absolutely baffling that this urban legend has obtained such traction,” Birch  wrote. “He said that it might be something to do with people misunderstanding the use of hashes to protect the integrity of data in the Estonian system.”
 Estonia’s technology vendor, Guardtime, rebranded its offering from “ hash-linked time-stamping” to a “blockchain technology.” That’s not necessarily untrue since “blockchain” has no agreed-upon definition — and for now, it’s a  good marketing tactic.
 “I asked him where this ‘Estonian blockchain ID’ myth came from since I find it absolutely baffling that this urban legend has obtained such traction.”
 “We have been working on the topic long, long before Bitcoin was thought of,” Mike Gault, the CEO of Guardtime, said in an email. “There is no new cryptography in Bitcoin — the genius behind it was taking different cryptographic building blocks and building a cryptocurrency protocol that incentivizes people to use it.”
 Blockchain is “an append-only data structure that contains data records that are cryptographically linked together,” he said. “Data records are added to the data structure when multiple distributed parties come to consensus based on pre-agreed rules.”
  A significant chunk of new blockchain proposals, like those proposed for the financial industry, are so-called “private” blockchains. Critics say these projects are old technology masquerading as something new.
 “‘Private blockchain’ is just a confusing name for a shared database,”  wrote Arvind Narayanan, an assistant computer science professor at Princeton who co-teaches a popular blockchain class on Coursera.
 Narayanan argues that the key innovation behind Bitcoin’s blockchain was the so-called proof-of-work consensus mechanism, which was intended to replace the need for a central authority with rules and incentives that would keep members of the network honest. Proof of work is inefficient and is the reason Bitcoin’s network  consumes so much energy, so it’s not necessarily a bad thing to ditch it. But without proof of work, is there anything really new about blockchains?
 “‘Private blockchain’ is just a confusing name for a shared database.”
 Some would cite other cryptographic techniques as being the distinction between a blockchain and “a vanilla shared database,” Narayanan wrote, but those techniques are nothing new. “The crypto makes the system harder to tamper with and easier to audit,” he wrote. “But these aspects of the blockchain weren’t Bitcoin’s innovation! In fact, Satoshi tweaked them only slightly from the earlier research that he cites in his white paper — research by Haber and Stornetta going all the way back to 1991!”
  This uncertainty has contributed to the general bubbliness of the industry by inflating the number of “blockchain” projects and exaggerating the capabilities of the technology. It may also cause unpredictable problems in the future as states pass blockchain-related legislation.
 Angela Walch, an associate professor at St. Mary’s University School of Law and research fellow at the Centre for Blockchain Technologies at University College London, wrote a paper about  blockchain-related terminology and the law.
 “A bunch of states are really in a rush to pass some sort of legislation to demonstrate how crypto-friendly or tech-savvy they are,” she said. “Many of them are putting definitions of blockchain technology in these statutes, and from my perspective, they are very problematic definitions.”
 The definition that concerns Walch the most is the one developed by the state of Arizona. Arizona’s Electronic Transactions Act was amended in 2017 to clarify that it covers transactions done on a blockchain. In doing so, the legislature wrote a definition: “‘Blockchain technology’ means distributed ledger technology that uses a distributed, decentralized, shared and replicated ledger, which may be public or private, permissioned or permissionless, or driven by tokenized crypto economics or tokenless. The data on the ledger is protected with cryptography, is immutable and auditable and provides an uncensored truth.”
 Just because the data is in a blockchain doesn’t mean the data is accurate
 In particular, Walch is concerned about the phrases “immutable” and “uncensored truth,” which ascribe absolutes to a technology that may be better described as “hard to change” and “uncensored as long as the people maintaining the network, who may include  miners,  developers, or  dictators, want it to be.” Gideon Greenspan, founder of Coin Sciences, wrote that the cost of rewriting the Bitcoin blockchain is  well within reach for a motivated nation-state. There are many highly publicized instances of blockchains being altered: Bitcoin was  forked multiple times, including in 2010 when an “integer overflow” error in the software led to the creation of 92 billion bitcoins and the entire network had to roll back the ledger.  Ethereum was forked after a massive hack in 2016. Furthermore, due to Europe’s  General Data Protection Regulation, which comes into effect in May and says users must have control over their data, developers are now exploring ways to  delete data from blockchains.
 The phrase “uncensored truth” also ignores the fact that just because the data is in a blockchain doesn’t mean the data is accurate. Inaccurate data, such as a mistake on a medical record, can still be validated in a blockchain.
 “What is a court supposed to do later when the definition there has no resemblance to the technology? And what are the implications for that from a legal perspective?” Walch said. “Things can get very messy.”
 To make things worse, Arizona’s definition is now being used for proposed legislation in other states  including California. Blockchain designs have also been proposed by  over 200 governments for use in various applications including voting, property records, and digital identity.
  Victoria Lemieux, an associate professor of archival science and head of the blockchain research cluster at the University of British Columbia, is leading the  effort to develop a blockchain terminology standard for the International Standards Organization.
 “In general, if the transactions are gathered together in blocks, and it is blocks that are secured on the chain using cryptography, and it is designed to be tamper-resistant and produce immutable records, the system qualifies as a blockchain,” she said in an email. “That said, in general usage, blockchain is often a term that encompasses a broad range of distributed ledgers, even if transactions are not organized into blocks.”
  Her team has run into some challenges, including the fact that “different epistemic communities have formed their own ideas about what blockchain is, some with very strong political and social views around open source, sharing, and autonomy.” These communities are not well-integrated into the ISO process, she said, and many members feel they are being overshadowed by large tech firms and other commercial interests.
 Another challenge is the proliferation of legal definitions that “may mean that these jurisdictions are out of step, and complicate legal processes or application of the technology,” she said.
 Lemieux is also well-acquainted with misconceptions about the capabilities of blockchains. “The concept of trustworthiness — at least from an archival science perspective — goes far beyond what the blockchain can do, or even promises to do, in most cases,” she said. This idea implies that records are accurate, “which is not something typically in scope of a good number of blockchain solutions” and exaggerates their reliability, which is an “issue if you have poorly written smart contracts or novel and untested consensus algorithms.” It also exaggerates claims of authenticity, which relies on the robustness of whatever identity system is paired with the blockchain. “Finally, immutability implies permanence, and there’s no guarantee that ledger records created and kept on chain will last, even with lots of copies around, because of technological obsolescence and the fact that incentives to keep the system going may die off after a time,” she said.
 Establishing a clear definition will help clear up some of these misunderstandings. “Developing a more precise understanding of what blockchain technology is will help us address its shortcomings and improve upon it so that it can better be used to in the transformative ways that its proponents envision,” she said. “It’s difficult to have a conversation about advancing a technology or using it when we all mean different things when we speak about it.” Unfortunately, she estimates the terminology standard will take approximately 18 months to be finalized.

note:
由于大的Solarwinds Hack和最近描述了新依赖混乱攻击的博客文章，最近有很多关于供应链攻击的谈话，令人妥协的安全元素损害的偷偷摸摸的技术来危及更安全的目标。
 一种流行的供应链攻击目标始终是流行的编程语言的许多包管理系统，例如JavaScript，Rubys的RubyGem和Python的Pypi。这些系统多年来困扰着恶意演员上传恶意包裹并等待受害者安装它们。
 我没有听说过很多谈论情况如何寻找去生态系统，因为Go是我的新语言，我决定做一些调查。
 新颖的依赖混淆攻击矢量幸运的是，由于进口包在导入包时始终明确指定源，因此在获取外部依赖项时，源自依赖，因此无法困惑，因此无法对此进行烦恼，因此在哪里获取它：
  然而，通过简单的Typosquatting，攻击者在键盘上打字时，攻击者攻击的事实仍然是一个可能的攻击矢量。这两者都可以在主机域上完成，其中攻击者注册Github.com的常见错误，但更容易将在GitHub上注册新用户或任何其他打开的包主机，其中包含包所有者的常见拼写错误用户名。
 考虑到这一点，我旨在建造一个可以帮助我在野外揭开潜在类型的工具：
 使每个唯一包所有者的用户名置换为获取潜在类型的列表 
记录具有等于正在检查的原始软件包的名称的任何存储库
 这导致了一个新的工具，我称之为Gitlab上可用的PKGTWIST（如果您有兴趣做自己的邪恶去包狩猎，则该名称由Awesome DNSTWIST工具启发）。
  可能是Pkgtwist最重要的部分是良好的用户名排列，以获得最佳检测类型的机会。一些研究导致了我到Zntrio / typogenerater，它看起来像是用于生成潜在用户名字的完美包装。包装实现了一个很长的置换策略列表，即我从中挑选了一些人，以便PKGTWIST只花时间检查我的觉得最有可能的类型：
  这意味着如果pkgtwist给予包github.com/strettr/timedify作为输入，它将检查github上是否存在任何以下用户，如果他们这样做，请检查它们是否也有一个名为testify的存储库：

  接下来是找到要检查的包列表。最初，我想到了寻找某种“顶级X Go Packages”列表，但我真的无法找到一个很好的资源，所以我最终从Gathub.com和Gitlab.com托管包中运行了pkgtwist模块索引（全部731包）。
   经过几个小时的时间通过731封装，PKGTWIST完成了7个潜在的Typosquat包装清单，以进一步调查。老实说，预计列表要更大，但我当然很高兴看到Go生态系统并没有完全被侵染（尚未使用恶意的Typosquat包裹。 
Sirupsen的Logrus包是一个非常受欢迎的日志记录包（GitHub上的17.3k星级），许多Go Projects使用，这显然使其成为Typosquat供应链攻击的目标。因此，当我看到用户SiRuspen（注意字母交换）有类似命名的存储库时，我很快就会检查它正在做的事情。
 事实证明，该项目是原始Logrus存储库的叉子，因此比较要找到差异非常容易。在撰写本文时，唯一添加到潜在的typosquat存储库，是一个小型init函数，单个println呼叫：
  虽然这不是恶意以任何方式，但是在未来的所有者可以非常迅速地改变，所以我肯定会在这个存储库中留意。如果您使用Logrus以确保您不使用此包而不是真实的，我还建议推荐仔细检查项目。
  URFAVE / CLI是建立CLI项目的另一个受欢迎的GO包（GITHUB上的15.4k星星）。因此，当我看到用户UTFAVE也有一个名为CLI的存储库，我的闹钟铃声脱离了，我进一步调查了。
   它看起来是Utfave希望使用他们的URFave / CLI版本了解所有计算机的主机名，操作系统和体系结构。该函数提取系统信息，然后通过HTTP向中国公司腾讯计算机系统呼出到中国公司深圳腾讯计算机系统的IP地址122.51.124.140，并将系统信息添加为URL参数。
 虽然此代码不会给出任何对系统的访问权限，但它非常可疑，它们收集此信息，并且演员可以快速更改此代码，如果它们识别系统是有价值或有趣的，则可以快速更改此代码以调用反向外壳。
 我向Github报道了这个存储库，希望看到它在不久的将来下降。在此之前，我建议使用URFAVE / CLI来仔细检查项目，以确保您不使用typosquatted版本。 
虽然我的小型研究项目没有在那里覆盖每一个Go封装，但我觉得它足以给出到Go生态系统的供应链攻击情况的粗略图片。这两个存储库苏黎普鲁斯/洛格勒斯和utfave / cli是唯一一个从7标记的repos中唯一一个真正担心我的那些，但我会在其余的休息时保持紧密的眼睛，因为他们在任何时候都可以变得恶意。
 我认为Go是比其他编程语言更好的情况，因为每次使用时都是始终明确写入的包，但代码编辑器自动化可以使类型更可能发生的类型，因为开发人员无法手动编写导入路径。经常。例如，如果安装了VS代码的流行Go扩展，则开发人员通常只在第一个使用情况下键入包导入，然后在使用包名称时，编辑器将自动在任何其他文件中添加导入。如果开发人员在进口路径第一次出现进口路径时，可以在它发现之前在GO项目中引入恶意包并长时间地生活。
note_en:
Because of the big  SolarWinds hack and the recent blog post describing a new  Dependency Confusion attack, there has been a lot of talk lately about supply chain attacks, the sneaky technique of compromising less secure elements in a supply chain to compromise more secure targets.
 A popular target for supply chain attacks has always been the many package management systems for popular programming languages such as NPM for JavaScript, Rubygems for Ruby, and PyPI for Python. These systems have been plagued for years by malicious actors uploading malicious packages and waiting for victims to install them.
 I haven’t heard much talk about how the situation looks for the Go ecosystem, and since Go is my new language of choice, I decided to do some investigation.
 The novel  Dependency Confusion attack vector is luckily not something a Go developer has to worry about since the source is always explicitly specified when importing a package, so when Go fetches the external dependency, it can’t be confused about where to fetch it:
  There is however still a possible attack vector via simple  typosquatting where the attacker exploits the fact that people often hit the wrong keys when typing on their keyboard. This could both be done on the host domain where the attacker registers a common typo of github.com, but even easier would be to simply register a new user on GitHub, or any other open package host, with a common typo of the package owner’s username.
 With this in mind, I set out to build a tool that would help me uncover potential typosquat packages in the wild:
 Permutate the username of every unique package owner to get a list of potential typosquats
      Log any of the repositories that have a name equal to the original packages being checked
 This resulted in a new tool I call  pkgtwist which is  available on GitLab (the name is inspired by the awesome  dnstwist tool) if you are interested in doing your own evil Go package hunting.
  Probably the most important part of  pkgtwist was the generation of good username permutations in order to have the best chance of detecting typosquats. A bit of research led me to  zntrio/typogenerater which looked like the perfect package for generating potential username typos. The package implements a pretty long list of permutation strategies that I picked a few from so that pkgtwist only spends time on checking what I feel are the most likely typosquats:
  This means that if pkgtwist is given the package  github.com/stretchr/testify as input, it will check if any of the of following users exist on GitHub, and if they do, check if they have also have a repository called  testify:
 tretchr  sretchr  stetchr  strtchr  strechr  strethr  stretcr  stretch  sstretchr  sttretchr  strretchr  streetchr  strettchr  stretcchr  stretchhr  stretchrr  rtretchr  qtretchr  ptretchr  wtretchr  vtretchr  utretchr  ttretchr  suretchr  svretchr  swretchr  spretchr  sqretchr  srretchr  ssretchr  stsetchr  stpetchr  stqetchr  stvetchr  stwetchr  sttetchr  stuetchr  strdtchr  strgtchr  strftchr  stratchr  strctchr  strbtchr  streuchr  strevchr  strewchr  strepchr  streqchr  strerchr  streschr  stretbhr  stretahr  stretghr  stretfhr  stretehr  stretdhr  stretcir  stretcjr  stretckr  stretclr  stretcmr  stretcnr  stretcor  stretchs  stretchp  stretchq  stretchv  stretchw  stretcht  stretchu  tsretchr  srtetchr  stertchr  strtechr  strecthr  strethcr  stretcrh
  Next up was to find a list of packages to check. Initially, I thought about finding some sort of “Top X Go packages” list, but I couldn’t really find a good resource for that, so I ended up running pkgtwist against every github.com and gitlab.com hosted packages from the  Go Module Index (731 packages in all).
   After several hours of crunching through the 731 packages, pkgtwist finished with a list of 7 potential typosquat packages to investigate further. I honestly expected the list to be bigger, but I was of course happy to see that the Go ecosystem isn’t completely infested (yet) with malicious typosquat packages.
   The  logrus package by  sirupsen is a very popular logging package (17.3k stars on GitHub) that many Go projects use, which obviously makes it a target for a typosquat supply chain attack. So when I saw that the user  siruspen (notice the letter swapping) had a similarly named repository, I quickly checked out what it was doing.
 It turns out that the project is a fork of the original  logrus repository, so doing a comparison to find the differences was pretty easy. At the time of writing, the only thing that is added to the potential typosquat repository,  is a small init function with a single  Println call:
  While this is not malicious in any way it could very quickly be changed by the owner in the future, so I will definitely keep an eye on this repository. I would also recommend double-checking your projects if you use  logrus to make sure you’re not using this package instead of the real one!
  urfave/cli is another popular Go package (15.4k stars on GitHub) for building CLI projects. So when I saw the user  utfave also had a repository called  cli, my alarm bells went off and I investigated further.
   It looks like the author  utfave wants to know the hostname, operating system, and architecture of all the machines using their version of  urfave/cli. The function extracts the system information and then calls out to the IP address  122.51.124.140 belonging to the Chinese company Shenzhen Tencent Computer Systems via HTTP with the system information added as URL parameters.
 While this code won’t give them any access to systems, it’s highly suspicious that they collect this information and the actor can quickly change this code to call back with a reverse shell if they identify a system to be valuable or interesting.
 I reported this repository to GitHub and hope to see it taken down in the near future. Until then, I recommend double-checking your projects if you use the  urfave/cli to make sure you’re not using the typosquatted version.
  While my little research project didn’t cover every single Go package out there, I feel it covered enough to give a rough picture of what the supply chain attack situation looks like for the Go ecosystem. The two repositories  siruspen/logrus and  utfave/cli, were the only ones out of the 7 flagged repos that really worried me, but I will keep a close eye on the rest as they could in theory become malicious at any time.
 I think Go is in a better situation than other programming languages because the source of packages is always explicitly written every time they are used, but code editor automation could make typosquat attacks more likely to happen as the developer doesn’t write the import paths manually as often. As an example, if the popular  Go extension for  VS Code is installed, a developer will typically only type a package import on the first usage, and then the editor will automatically add the import in any other files as soon as the package name is used. If the developer mistypes the import path the first time, a malicious package could be introduced and live for a long time in a Go project before it’s discovered.

除非您住在北卡罗来纳州亨斯维尔，否则您可能会幸福地不知道，除非我们自1997年以来的最大汽油溢出发生了。州慢慢展开，州的佐贺队涉及一家由像Koch兄弟和贝壳这样的特殊利益控制的公司，以及一直运输数十年的管道。以及殖民地管道的危机指出了美国化石燃料基础设施的下一个大问题之一：当我们搬到清洁能源时，危险，老化管道有关。
    8月份，两个骑马在夏洛特北部的郊区亨斯维尔外的自然保存的两个少年，注意到汽油咕噜地从地面上拿出来告诉城镇消防部门。 （殖民主人的主人告诉一些国家立法者一个不同的故事，最初声称他们在注意到这条线上的另一个点的压力下降后他们会关闭管道。）一开始，管道公司报告只有63,000加仑的石油溢出但是，整个秋季，该数字稳步悄悄爬升：11月份9月份的273,000加仑，111万加仑。公司现在表示近120万加仑泄露;随着更多的评估，该数字仍然可以上升。
 虽然管道业主倾向于疲劳的溢出初期，但这种螺旋水平是“有点天文而且不正常”，这是生物多样性中心的高级律师Jared Margolis说。 MARGOLIS表示，汽油的物理成分 - 它沉入水体底部，与石油不同，它在顶部保持光滑 - 可能会使溢出物如此难以衡量。
  上个月在北达科他州溢出超过383,000加仑的石油后，Keystone管道......
